display_title: "I Am a Machine": Programming and Control in Dick's Exegesis (PKD Festival 2024)
author: Gabriel McKee
card_summary: A PDF export of a slide presentation delivered by Gabriel McKee at the Philip K. Dick Festival on June 14, 2024. The talk, titled 'I Am a Machine: Programming and Control in Dick's Exegesis,' examines themes of mechanical determinism and control within Dick's Exegesis. Slides reference the Zebrapedia Project transcription archive, Exegesis folders 82, 34, 22, and 90, a letter from PKD to Claudia Bush (July 30, 1974), the first German edition of VALIS (Moewig, 1984), and imagery from Athanasius Kircher's Ars Magna Lucis et Umbrae (1646).
page_summary: This document is a PDF export of a slide presentation delivered by Gabriel McKee at the Philip K. Dick Festival on June 14, 2024. The talk, titled 'I Am a Machine: Programming and Control in Dick's Exegesis,' examines themes of mechanical determinism, programming, and control as they appear throughout Dick's Exegesis, his massive private philosophical and theological journal. The slides reference specific Exegesis folders -- folders 82, 34, 22, and 90 -- indicating that McKee engaged in close reading of particular passages across multiple sections of the unpublished manuscript. All Exegesis images used in the presentation are credited to the Zebrapedia Project, a collaborative transcription initiative hosted on FromThePage.com that is working to make the handwritten Exegesis digitally accessible. The presentation also references a letter from Dick to Claudia Bush dated July 30, 1974, situating Dick's reflections on determinism and mechanical consciousness within his early post-2-3-74 correspondence. Visual materials in the slides include the first German edition of VALIS, published by Moewig in Rastatt, Germany, in 1984, with translation credited to Helmut Wenske, and an engraving by Pierre Miotte from Athanasius Kircher's Ars Magna Lucis et Umbrae (1646) depicting the effects of planets on parts of the body, sourced from the Wellcome Collection. The inclusion of Kircher's seventeenth-century illustration of astrological-anatomical correspondences suggests McKee drew thematic parallels between early modern conceptions of cosmic determinism and Dick's own explorations of whether human beings are 'programmed' by external forces. The presentation represents contemporary scholarly engagement with Dick's Exegesis at a community-oriented festival setting, bridging academic analysis with fan scholarship. McKee's focus on the machine metaphor in the Exegesis connects to broader themes in Dick's fiction concerning the boundaries between humans and automata, free will and determinism, and authentic versus counterfeit consciousness.

filename: PKD Correspondence The Dark Haired Girl.pdf
display_title: A Dark-Haired Girl & PKD: Letters from the Heart, the Spleen, and the Funnybone
author: Philip K. Dick; Linda Levy; compiled by Linda Castellani
card_summary: A collection of correspondence between Philip K. Dick and Linda Levy, compiled and edited by Linda Castellani (copyright 2008). The volume is subtitled 'Letters from the Heart, the Spleen, and the Funnybone,' reflecting the emotional and humorous range of the exchange. The 51-page document is organized into sections covering what the collection contains, a preface, and three labeled sides attributed to PKD, suggesting a structured editorial arrangement of letters across different phases or themes of the correspondence.
page_summary: This fifty-one-page document, compiled and edited by Linda Castellani (copyright 2008), presents the correspondence between Philip K. Dick and Linda Levy under the title 'A Dark-Haired Girl & PKD: Letters from the Heart, the Spleen, and the Funnybone.' Castellani, who is Linda Levy, structured the collection into sections labeled 'PKD, Side One' through 'PKD, Side Four,' plus 'Odds and Ends,' each representing different phases or thematic dimensions of the correspondence. The preface establishes the collection as the first offered for sale by one of Dick's 'dark-haired girls,' with Castellani presenting the original pieces of paper on which the correspondence was written, supplemented by contextual narrative. Each letter is shown with one page partially obscured by puzzle-piece imagery, chosen to reflect what Castellani calls 'the puzzle that was the man himself.' Beyond the letters, the collection includes Castellani's personal copy of The Dark-Haired Girl, two Georg Grosz prints signed by Grosz in pencil, the only known prints of three photographs Dick took of Castellani, and the November 6, 1975 issue of Rolling Stone magazine containing an article on Dick that he sent her a dollar to purchase. The preface references a Paul Giamatti biopic then in development and expresses Castellani's desire to present her account before the film version solidified public perception. She describes Dick as multidimensional, capable of love and longing, meanness and vindictiveness, playfulness and humor, and a brooding darkness that led to outbursts of physical and emotional violence toward women he professed to love. The document quotes from Dick's December 1, 1972 letter to Roger Zelazny, in which Dick describes his search for the dark-haired girl and mentions compiling 127 pages of letters sent to his agent as a sort of journal, originally titled 'The Dark-Haired Girl: A Search for the Authentic Human Being.' The collection provides a unique first-person perspective on Dick's personal relationships during the early 1970s, a period of intense personal upheaval and creative productivity.

display_title: A Life of Philip K. Dick: The Man Who Remembered the Future
author: Anthony Peake
card_summary: A biographical work on Philip K. Dick by Anthony Peake, subtitled 'The Man Who Remembered the Future.' Peake, known for his interest in consciousness and paranormal phenomena, examines Dick's life through the lens of his extraordinary mystical experiences, particularly the events of February 1974. The biography explores Dick's personal history, his prodigious fiction output, his drug use, his marriages, and his ongoing philosophical and theological investigations documented in the Exegesis.
page_summary:
A Life of Philip K. Dick: The Man Who Remembered the Future is a biographical work by Anthony Peake examining the life and experiences of Philip K. Dick. Peake, an author known for his investigations into consciousness, near-death experiences, and paranormal phenomena, brings a distinctive perspective to Dick's biography by taking seriously the extraordinary mystical and visionary experiences that dominated the final decade of Dick's life.

The subtitle signals Peake's particular interest in Dick's apparent precognitive abilities and his claims of accessing information beyond normal sensory channels. This positions the biography within the tradition of examining Dick not merely as a literary figure but as someone whose personal experiences of altered consciousness directly informed his fiction.

The text excerpt available is minimal (only 90 characters from a digital edition), containing only the title page information. The biography covers Dick's personal history, his prodigious fiction output spanning over 40 novels and 120 short stories, his troubled personal life including five marriages, his documented drug use, and his ongoing philosophical and theological investigations as recorded in the Exegesis.

display_title: A Scanner Darkly (Screenplay, First Draft)
author: Charlie Kaufman
card_summary: First draft screenplay dated December 20, 1997, written by Charlie Kaufman and adapted from Philip K. Dick's novel A Scanner Darkly. The script opens in Orange County, California in the year 1994 and follows Bob Arctor, depicting the same hallucinatory drug underworld as the source novel. Kaufman's adaptation precedes the 2006 Richard Linklater animated film. This document is a working draft, not a PKD-authored work, and represents an important piece of the film adaptation history of Dick's fiction.
page_summary:
This is the first draft screenplay adaptation of Philip K. Dick's novel A Scanner Darkly, written by Charlie Kaufman and dated December 20, 1997. Kaufman, who would later become renowned for screenplays including Being John Malkovich, Adaptation, and Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind, adapted Dick's semi-autobiographical novel about undercover narcotics agent Bob Arctor.

The screenplay opens with a shot of a notebook page dappled with sunlight, establishing a visual and narrative approach that reflects Kaufman's characteristically inventive storytelling methods. This is a significant document in the history of Dick film adaptations, as Kaufman's version was never produced. The film that was eventually made in 2006 was directed by Richard Linklater using a different screenplay and the distinctive rotoscoping animation technique.

Kaufman's unproduced adaptation represents an intriguing counterfactual in Dick cinema history, combining two of the most distinctive creative voices in American storytelling. The screenplay is a valuable document for scholars interested in the process of adapting Dick's work for film and in Kaufman's early career development.

Organized by the Friends of Armadillo fan group (FACT) based in Austin, Texas. page_summary:
The ArmadilloCon 10 Program Book is the official 36-page program for Austin's premiere science fiction convention, held October 11-13, 1989 at the Wyndham South hotel. The convention was sponsored by the Fandom Association of Central Texas (FACT), a 501(c)(3) nonprofit literary and educational organization based in Austin.

Guests of Honor included author Lewis Shiner (Deserted Cities of the Heart), Hugo-winning fan writer and File 770 editor Mike Glyer, Hugo-nominated artist Teddy Harvia (David Thayer, also credited for Wingnut Soccer), Doubleday Books editor Pat LoBrutto, and toastmaster Connie Willis (Lincoln's Dreams, Fire Watch). The program book also advertised advance registration for the following year's event at $10.

The document additionally references ArmadilloCon's previous year (1988) with Guest of Honor K.W. Jeter, fan guests Jane and Scott Dennis, toastmaster Lewis Shiner, artist guest Brad W. Foster, and editor guest Ginjer Buchanan. K.W. Jeter is particularly relevant to PKD studies as a close friend of Dick's and a fellow science fiction author who coined the term "steampunk." The ArmadilloCon program book documents the Texas science fiction fan community and its connections to writers in Dick's orbit.

The table of contents includes PKD stories such as Exhibit Piece alongside many other authors. If magazine was a prominent science fiction digest that published Dick and many other SF writers. This is a multi-author reprint anthology rather than a dedicated Philip K. Dick collection.
page_summary:
Despite its filename suggesting a Philip K. Dick anthology, this document is actually Best of If Magazine, Volume 1, a 2021 anthology compiled by Christopher Broschell collecting classic stories from If: Worlds of Science Fiction magazine. Dick's story "Exhibit Piece" appears in the table of contents alongside works by numerous other authors.

If magazine was a prominent American science fiction digest published from 1952 to 1974, winning the Hugo Award for Best Professional Magazine three years running (1966-1968). Other stories include J.F. Bone's "Assassin," along with works such as "The Six Fingers of Time," "Spawning Ground," and Kurt Vonnegut's "2BRO2B."

This volume is relevant to PKD research as a document of the publishing ecosystem in which Dick's short fiction appeared. If magazine, alongside Galaxy, The Magazine of Fantasy & Science Fiction, and Astounding, formed the core of the American SF magazine market that sustained Dick's career during the 1950s and 1960s.

author: Eaton Science Fiction Conference Program Committee
card_summary: The official Call for Papers for the 2015 Eaton Science Fiction Conference, titled "Designing the Past, Designing the Future," held October 16-17, 2015 at the Riverside Marriott Hotel in Riverside, California. The CFP solicits paper and panel proposals examining the relationship between speculative genres (science fiction, fantasy, horror, utopian literature) and the concept of design. page_summary:
This document is the official Call for Papers for the 2015 Eaton Science Fiction Conference, titled "Designing the Past, Designing the Future," held October 16-17, 2015 at the Riverside Marriott Hotel in Riverside, California. The Eaton Conference is one of the premier academic conferences devoted to science fiction scholarship.

The CFP solicits paper and panel proposals examining the relationship between speculative genres and the concept of design. Topics include images of engineers and engineering in SF, design practices in SF film and television, expositions and world's fairs, SF and the built environment, streamlining in pulp-era design, architectural utopias, the look of videogames and virtual spaces, retrofuturism and steampunk, SF design in an era of media convergence, and philosophies of design as SF theory.

The Eaton Conference is associated with the Eaton Collection of Science Fiction and Fantasy at UC Riverside, one of the world's largest collections of science fiction and fantasy materials. While not exclusively focused on Dick, the conference represents the kind of institutional academic engagement with science fiction that has elevated Dick's work to canonical status. The conference's location at UC Riverside connects it to the same institution that houses the Eric Foss Hour 25 collection and other materials relevant to Dick studies.

filename: generate a comprehensive audit of every documented.pdf
display_title: Comprehensive Audit of Philip K. Dick's Documented Public Appearances, Speeches, and Interviews
author: Unknown (compiled research document)
card_summary: A compiled audit of Philip K. Dick's documented public appearances, speeches, and interviews, drawing on philipidick.com archives, Frank C. Bertrand's research on missing or problem interviews, The Last Interview anthology, and fan scholarship databases. Key findings include that Dick's public-facing activity was concentrated between 1972 and 1982, with only three formal speeches documented: "The Android and the Human" (Vancouver, 1972), "If You Find This World Bad" (Metz, 1977), and "How to Build a Universe" (1978). His only international trip is also noted.
page_summary:
This document is a comprehensive audit of Philip K. Dick's documented public appearances, speeches, and interviews, compiled from multiple primary sources including the official philipdick.com archives, Frank C. Bertrand's research on missing or problem interviews, The Last Interview and Other Conversations anthology, and various fan scholarship databases.

Key findings establish that Dick was profoundly reclusive, with nearly all of his public-facing life occurring between 1972 and 1982. Virtually nothing is documented from the 1960s when he wrote his greatest works. Only three formal speeches are documented: "The Android and the Human" delivered at the Vancouver SF Convention in 1972, "If You Find This World Bad, You Should See Some of the Others" delivered at the Metz Science Fiction Festival in France in 1977, and "How to Build a Universe That Doesn't Fall Apart Two Days Later" from 1978, which may never have been delivered.

Dick's only international trip was to the Metz Science Fiction Festival in September 1977, which generated the densest cluster of recorded appearances. The most obscure appearances include a Norwegian journalist's 1971 visit and a 1972 three-part appearance. This audit is an invaluable reference for researchers seeking to understand the full scope of Dick's engagement with the public and the literary community.

display_title: Cosmogony and Cosmology
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A 1978 philosophical essay by Philip K. Dick in which he develops his concept of reality as a projected framework generated by an artifact he calls Zebra, a computerlike teaching machine. Dick argues that this artifact projects our world as a mirror for its maker (the Urgrund, a term borrowed from Jakob Boehme, 1616) to achieve self-awareness and self-knowledge. The essay is a key document in understanding Dick's late metaphysical and theological thinking, closely related to his ongoing Exegesis project.
page_summary:
"Cosmogony and Cosmology" is a 1978 philosophical essay by Philip K. Dick that presents one of his most systematic attempts to articulate the metaphysical framework he developed following his 2-3-74 mystical experiences. The essay centers on Dick's concept of reality as a projected framework generated by an artifact he calls Zebra.

Dick argues that Zebra is a computerlike teaching machine that guides, programs, and controls humanity within a projected world. This artifact was constructed by a vast living organism Dick calls the Urgrund, borrowing the term from the German mystic Jakob Boehme (1616). The Urgrund, Dick explains, is intrinsically without qualities or aspects and requires an empirical world as a mirror by which to achieve self-awareness and self-knowledge. The projected reality thus serves as an instrument through which the Urgrund can obtain an objective standpoint to comprehend its own nature.

The artifact itself is unaware that it is an artifact and is oblivious to the existence of the Urgrund. This layered ontology, in which the creator, the demiurge, and the created world exist in a hierarchy of awareness, draws explicitly on Platonic and Gnostic philosophy. The essay is a key document in Dick's late metaphysical thought, closely related to his Exegesis project, and represents his most direct engagement with systematic cosmological speculation. It provides essential context for understanding the theological framework underlying his VALIS trilogy.

filename: Divine Invasions_ A Life of Phi - Lawrence Sutin.pdf
display_title: Divine Invasions: A Life of Philip K. Dick
author: Lawrence Sutin
card_summary: The full text of Lawrence Sutin's landmark biography of Philip K. Dick, "Divine Invasions: A Life of Philip K. Dick." Dedicated to the author's parents Jack and Rochelle Sutin, the book is described by Sutin as an adventure beyond initial expectations. Sutin credits agent Dorothy Pittman for marketing a proposal that industry insiders considered commercially unviable. At 554 pages, this is the definitive long-form biographical account of Dick's life and remains a foundational reference for PKD scholarship.
page_summary:
Divine Invasions: A Life of Philip K. Dick is Lawrence Sutin's landmark biography, widely regarded as the definitive account of Dick's life. The book is dedicated to Sutin's parents, Jack and Rochelle Sutin, and opens with acknowledgments that illuminate the challenges of writing a comprehensive Dick biography.

Sutin describes the project as an adventure beyond initial expectations, noting that those who regard Dick's explorations of reality as mere metaphysical quibbling have never undertaken to write a full account of his life. He credits his agent, Dorothy Pittman, who diligently marketed the proposal when industry insiders declared a Dick biography commercially impossible, and his editor, Michael Pietsch, who took the risk of purchasing the book. Paul Williams, literary executor of the Dick Estate, provided full cooperation with the sole concern that the truth be told. Anne Dick is also acknowledged as a key source.

At 554 pages, the biography draws on extensive interviews, archival research, and Dick's own writings to reconstruct a life marked by extraordinary creative productivity, personal turmoil, philosophical obsession, and mystical experience. Sutin's work established the biographical framework within which virtually all subsequent Dick scholarship has operated, and his careful, sympathetic treatment of both Dick's literary achievements and personal difficulties set the standard for Dick biography.

display_title: Electric Dreams
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A collection of ten Philip K. Dick short stories published in conjunction with the Electric Dreams television anthology series. Each story is preceded by an introduction from the respective TV episode's screenwriter or producer, including Ronald D. Moore, Jack Thorne, David Farr, Dee Rees, and others. Stories included are: Exhibit Piece, The Commuter, The Impossible Planet, The Hanging Stranger, Sales Pitch, The Father-Thing, The Hood Maker, Foster You're Dead, Human Is, and Autofac.
page_summary:
Electric Dreams is a short story collection by Philip K. Dick published in conjunction with the Channel 4/Amazon television anthology series of the same name. The collection gathers ten Dick stories, each preceded by an introduction from the respective television episode's screenwriter or producer.

The stories included are: Exhibit Piece (introduced by Ronald D. Moore), The Commuter (introduced by Jack Thorne), The Impossible Planet (introduced by David Farr), The Hanging Stranger (introduced by Dee Rees), Sales Pitch (introduced by Tony Grisoni), The Father-Thing (introduced by Michael Dinner), The Hood Maker (introduced by Matthew Graham), Foster, You're Dead (introduced by Kalen Egan and Travis Sentell), Human Is (introduced by Jessica Mecklenburg), and Autofac (introduced by Travis Beacham).

These stories span Dick's most productive short fiction period of the 1950s and early 1960s and represent several recurring themes: the instability of everyday suburban reality, Cold War paranoia, the question of authentic humanity, and technology's encroachment on human autonomy. The collection serves as both a companion to the television series and an accessible entry point to Dick's short fiction.

Poetry from Heinrich Heine and references to Goethe's Faust and Beethoven's Fidelio are also credited.

The opening scene introduces a man standing all day shaking bugs from his hair that a doctor insists do not exist, immediately establishing the novel's central themes of perceptual unreliability, drug-induced psychosis, and the dissolution of identity. A Scanner Darkly is widely regarded as one of Dick's finest achievements, drawing directly on his own experiences in the drug culture of 1970s Southern California.

The transcript includes commentary on photographs of Dick, including images of him with cats (reflecting his well-known love of felines), a photograph of his grave, and a late photograph taken with director Ridley Scott during the editing of Blade Runner. One speaker recounts the pet shop where Dick reportedly purchased horse meat intended for animals to feed himself during periods of severe financial hardship. The document references publisher Daniel Rich, who is described as possessing rare materials on Dick, including an exceptional recorded interview from 1972. A brief English-language fragment attributed to Dick is embedded in the French text, in which he describes his mother's decision to sell his house, leaving him with nowhere to go during a period of economic difficulty. The transcript provides biographical data, noting Dick's birth on December 16, 1928, in Chicago, his lifelong residence in California, his five marriages, and his death in 1982 from a cerebral attack just as Hollywood was beginning to discover his work through Blade Runner. Speakers describe Dick as a visionary writer who stood apart from traditional science fiction, calling him a prophet and a 'world-zapper.' A significant portion of the transcript discusses Ubik, with speakers describing how the novel prefigured the excesses of globalization through its depiction of an omnipresent brand. The designer Philippe Starck is mentioned as having created a company called UBC and named products after Dick's characters, including Joe Chip, Doctor Bloodmoney, and Lucinda Carter. Artist Vincent Bergera is discussed for creating actual 'Ubik' branded products that blur the line between Dick's fiction and consumer reality. The transcript conveys the particularly strong French reception of Dick's work and the cultural seriousness with which French intellectuals and artists have engaged his literary legacy.

display_title: How to Build a Universe That Doesn't Fall Apart Two Days Later
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A celebrated 1978 essay and speech by Philip K. Dick in which he explores questions of reality, authenticity, and what it means for things and people to be real versus fake. Originally delivered as a speech, Dick weaves together personal anecdotes, philosophical reflection, and science-fictional speculation. He discusses Disneyland, fascism, and the nature of the universe with characteristic wit and depth. A key primary text for understanding his philosophical worldview.
page_summary:
How to Build a Universe That Doesn't Fall Apart Two Days Later is a celebrated 1978 essay by Philip K. Dick, originally delivered as a speech, in which he addresses his two central preoccupations as a writer: the question of what is real and the question of what constitutes an authentic human being. The essay opens with a characteristically self-deprecating anecdote about being interviewed by Paris TV at Disneyland alongside Norman Spinrad, where they discussed the rise of fascism while riding in giant teacups.

Dick uses this absurdist scene as a launching point for a wide-ranging philosophical meditation. He describes how his entire body of writing has been driven by the suspicion that the universe we inhabit may not be real, and that the people around us may not be genuinely human in any meaningful sense. He draws on examples from consumer culture, political manipulation, and his own fiction to explore how fake realities are manufactured.

The essay is significant as one of Dick's most articulate and accessible statements of his philosophical worldview. Unlike the dense, circuitous explorations of the Exegesis, this piece presents his core ideas in a polished, public-facing format. It has become one of the most frequently cited and anthologized of Dick's non-fiction works, serving as an essential introduction to his thinking about simulation, authenticity, and the constructed nature of consensus reality.

page_summary:
How to Build an Android: The True Story of Philip K. Dick's Robotic Resurrection is a non-fiction book by David F. Dufty chronicling the creation of a lifelike android replica of Philip K. Dick by Hanson Robotics in collaboration with researchers at the University of Memphis.

The book is structured as narrative non-fiction spanning sixteen chapters and an epilogue. The chapter titles trace a compelling arc: from conception ("A Strange Machine," "A Tale of Two Researchers") through technical challenges ("How to Build a Human Head," "Life Inside a Laptop") to the android's public life ("An Android in Memphis," "First, We Take Chicago") and its mysterious end ("Brain Malfunction," "Headless").

The project brought together roboticist David Hanson, AI researchers, and Dick scholars to create a conversational android modeled on Dick's appearance and trained on his writings. Chapter 9, "A California Bungalow, 1974," suggests the book also explores Dick's 2-3-74 experiences. The android famously went missing in 2006 after being left in an overhead compartment on a flight, an event with almost Dickian irony. The book explores themes central to Dick's own fiction: consciousness, the human-machine boundary, and what it means to resurrect a person through technology.

display_title: In Pursuit of VALIS: Selections from the Exegesis
author: Philip K. Dick (edited by Lawrence Sutin)
card_summary: An Underwood-Miller anthology published in 1991 collecting selections from Philip K. Dick's vast private philosophical journal known as the Exegesis. Edited by Lawrence Sutin, with a preface by Sutin, an afterword by Terence McKenna, and an introduction by Jay Kinney. Also includes a PKD chronology by Paul Williams. The Exegesis documents Dick's intense mystical experiences beginning in February 1974 and his ongoing attempts to understand their theological and philosophical significance.
page_summary:
In Pursuit of VALIS: Selections from the Exegesis is a landmark 1991 anthology published by Underwood-Miller of Novato, California, collecting edited excerpts from Philip K. Dick's massive private philosophical journal known as the Exegesis. Edited by Lawrence Sutin, the volume features a preface by Sutin, an introduction by Jay Kinney (a shorter version of which appeared in Gnosis Magazine, issue 1, Fall 1985), and an afterword by Terence McKenna. A PKD Chronology compiled by Paul Williams is also included.

The Exegesis represents Dick's sustained attempt to interpret and understand a series of mystical and visionary experiences that began in February and March of 1974, commonly referred to among scholars as the "2-3-74" events. Over the remaining eight years of his life, Dick filled thousands of handwritten pages exploring theology, Gnostic philosophy, Neoplatonism, quantum physics, and the nature of reality in an effort to make sense of what he believed was a divine revelation.

This anthology was the first major published selection from the Exegesis and served for decades as the primary access point for scholars and readers interested in Dick's late-period metaphysical thought. The contributors represent key figures in Dick studies: Sutin authored the definitive Dick biography Divine Invasions, Williams founded the Philip K. Dick Society, Kinney edited Gnosis Magazine, and McKenna was a prominent figure in psychedelic and esoteric discourse. Published in both clothbound (ISBN 0-88733-091-6) and softcover (ISBN 0-88733-093-2) editions.

The experiment uses superconducting transmon qubits to demonstrate wavefunction collapse consistent with Orch-OR. The organization name 'Valis Corporation' directly references PKD's novel VALIS, linking this modern quantum consciousness research to Dick's philosophical preoccupations with the nature of reality and mind. 8 pages.
page_summary:
This scientific paper by James Tagg and William Reid of Valis Corporation (Encinitas, California), dated April 2, 2025, describes a quantum computing experiment testing Orchestrated Objective Reduction (Orch-OR), the theory of consciousness proposed by Sir Roger Penrose and Stuart Hameroff.

The experiment uses superconducting transmon qubits to demonstrate wavefunction collapse consistent with Orch-OR. The researchers perform a partial measurement on a qubit and use the result to move an estimated 10^-12 kg of mass in one of two locations separated by approximately 1 mm. In standard quantum mechanics, the partial measurement leaves the system in an improper mixture that retains quantum coherence. According to Penrose's theory, improper mixtures can lead to gravitationally induced objective reduction.

The organization name "Valis Corporation" directly references Dick's 1981 novel VALIS, creating a remarkable link between contemporary quantum consciousness research and Dick's philosophical preoccupations with the nature of reality and mind. Dick's fiction and Exegesis writings frequently explored themes of information, consciousness, and the fundamental nature of reality that parallel the concerns of Penrose-Hameroff theory. This paper represents a striking instance of Dick's intellectual legacy extending into cutting-edge physics research.

display_title: Ontology and Ethics in the Writings of Philip K. Dick (PhD Thesis)
author: Andrew Mark Butler
card_summary: A doctoral thesis submitted to the University of Hull in October 1995 by Andrew Mark Butler (B.A. Hons), examining ontology and ethics across Philip K. Dick's body of work. The thesis is structured in at least two chapters: the first addresses realism and the fantastic in Dick's fiction, including literary realism, author-reader relations, and the 'New Wave'; the second explores ontology and its discontents, engaging with postmodernism, Fredric Jameson, and Dick's narrative viewpoint techniques. Analyzes works including In Milton Lumky Territory. A substantial work of 313 pages representing serious academic engagement with Dick's literary and philosophical output.
page_summary:
Ontology and Ethics in the Writings of Philip K. Dick is a doctoral thesis submitted to the University of Hull in October 1995 by Andrew Mark Butler (B.A. Hons). At 313 pages, it represents one of the earliest and most substantial works of academic scholarship devoted to Dick's literary and philosophical output.

The thesis is organized into multiple chapters addressing distinct aspects of Dick's work. Chapter One, titled "Possible maybes," examines realism and the fantastic in Dick's fiction, covering literary realism, author-reader relations, the science fiction genre, the "New Wave" movement, and a detailed case study of In Milton Lumky Territory exploring familiarization, character types, and genre boundaries. Chapter Two, "Life is turning into a Philip K. Dick Novel," addresses ontology and its discontents, engaging with philosophical realism, Dick's use of viewpoint to mediate access to the real, postmodern theory (including Fredric Jameson and Jean Baudrillard on Dick), cyberpunk, and Dick as a postmodern icon. Chapter Three, "Can a person hallucinate without being psychotic?," examines hallucinatory environments in works including Eye in the Sky and Flow My Tears, the Policeman Said, with attention to McCarthyism, Black politics, and the American Left.

Butler went on to become a prominent Dick scholar, publishing the Pocket Essentials critical guide to Dick and contributing to numerous academic discussions of his work. This thesis represents the foundational research behind much of his subsequent scholarly output.

display_title: Philip K. Dick
author: Andrew M. Butler
card_summary: A critical introduction to Philip K. Dick's life and work by scholar Andrew M. Butler. The text surveys Dick's major themes including fake realities, questions of identity, the nature of consciousness, and his exploration of counter-cultural ideas. Butler positions Dick as a uniquely brilliant SF writer who probed the landscapes of the mind decades ahead of his time. Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?, the basis for Blade Runner, is highlighted as his most widely known work. Aimed at readers new to Dick's fiction.
page_summary:
Philip K. Dick by Andrew M. Butler is a critical introduction to Dick's life and work, published as part of a pocket essentials series. Butler, Senior Lecturer in Media and Cultural Studies at Canterbury Christ Church University College, provides an accessible survey aimed at readers approaching Dick's fiction for the first time.

The text opens with provocative questions about Dick's identity and positions him as a writer who explored the landscapes of the mind at a time when most science fiction focused on conventional space adventure. Butler highlights Dick's signature themes: fake realities, questions about authentic human experience, and a world where psychiatrists come in suitcases and God speaks through cat food commercials.

Butler notes that Dick embodied the counter-culture a decade before the 1960s and identifies Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep? as his most widely known work. The volume reviews and analyzes each of Dick's novels and includes listings of additional critical resources. This compact guide represents the kind of accessible scholarship that has helped expand Dick's readership beyond the science fiction community into mainstream literary culture.

display_title: Philip K. Dick and the Umbrella of Light
author: Angus Taylor
card_summary: An early critical monograph on Philip K. Dick by Angus Taylor, originally published in 1975 by T-K Graphics, Baltimore, with illustrations by Cora Lee Healy. Parts of the work appeared earlier in the British journal Foundation (No. 4, July 1973) under the title 'Can God Fly? Can He Hold Out His Arms and Fly?' The work is structured in three parts examining monsters from outer and inner space, the willing extension of disbelief, and the concept of the 'umbrella of light.' This edition is a 2025 reformatted reissue with minor corrections. A significant early work of PKD literary criticism.
page_summary:
Philip K. Dick and the Umbrella of Light is a critical monograph by Angus Taylor, published by T-K Graphics of Baltimore in 1975, with illustrations by C. Lee Healy. Parts of the work appeared earlier in the British journal Foundation, No. 4, July 1973, in an article titled "Can God Fly? Can He Hold Out His Arms and Fly?"

This is one of the earliest book-length critical studies devoted to Dick's work, published during Dick's lifetime. Taylor's study examines Dick's fiction through the lens of his philosophical and theological concerns, with the title referencing the imagery of light and transcendence that pervades Dick's later work. The original Foundation article from 1973 places Taylor's criticism alongside other early academic engagements with Dick in British science fiction criticism.

T-K Graphics was a small press that published several science fiction critical works during the 1970s. Taylor's monograph represents a pioneering effort to treat Dick's work as worthy of sustained critical analysis at a time when science fiction was still struggling for academic respectability. The work is now a collector's item and an important document in the history of Dick criticism.

filename: Philip K. Dick Estate Legal Research.pdf
display_title: Philip K. Dick Estate Legal Research: IP Litigation, Copyright Renewal, and Commercialization Strategies (1982-2026)
author: Unknown (legal research document)
card_summary: A detailed legal research document examining the intellectual property management, copyright renewal discrepancies, and commercialization strategies of the Philip K. Dick Testamentary Trust and Electric Shepherd Productions from 1982 to 2026. The document analyzes post-mortem estate administration challenges for prolific authors, using Dick's fragmented corpus of novels, novellas, and short stories as a central case study. Topics include multi-generational IP protection, entertainment industry licensing, and legal complexities arising from Dick's highly fragmented literary estate. 20 pages.
page_summary: This twenty-page legal research document provides a granular, chronological analysis of the intellectual property management, litigation history, copyright renewal disputes, and commercialization strategies of the Philip K. Dick Testamentary Trust and Electric Shepherd Productions from 1982 to 2026. The document opens by establishing that Dick's vast literary corpus has served as the foundational architecture for billions of dollars in cinematic and television adaptations, yet his death intestate in California in 1982 created a highly complex legal and corporate structure. The two primary entities governing Dick's estate are identified as the Philip K. Dick Testamentary Trust, which functions as the holding and litigation vehicle on behalf of Dick's three statutory heirs -- Laura Archer Dick Coelho, Christopher Dick, and Isa Dick Hackett -- and Electric Shepherd Productions, the corporate arm dedicated to active media development and production. A major focus of the document is the vulnerability of Dick's copyrights under the Copyright Act of 1909, which imposed a rigid 28-year initial term requiring formal renewal registration during the 28th year. Because Dick died before the renewal windows opened for many of his most lucrative short stories, the administrative burden fell on a newly formed and initially disorganized Trust. The chaotic nature of the mid-century pulp science fiction publishing industry compounded this challenge, as stories sold to ephemeral magazines such as Orbit Science Fiction, If, Galaxy Science Fiction, and Planet Stories often involved poorly drafted contracts or handshake agreements that left copyright ownership ambiguous. The report examines federal docket entries in the U.S. District Court for the Central District of California and the Southern District of New York, tracing the estate's evolution from reactive defensive litigation over lapsed copyrights to a proactive integrated production model designed to secure continuous revenue through derivative works, brand management, and trademark enforcement. This document is a significant resource for understanding the post-mortem administration of a prolific author's literary estate and the multi-jurisdictional legal strategies required to maintain control over intellectual property vulnerable to public domain attrition.

display_title: Philip K. Dick Extant Fiction Chronology
author: Frank Hollander
card_summary: A fan-compiled chronology of Philip K. Dick's extant completed fiction, researched and sequenced by Frank Hollander between July and November 2012. The document establishes a coherent reading order for Dick's fiction, using the Scott Meredith Literary Agency (SMLA) submission date as its primary organizing principle. It acknowledges similarity to Lord RC's chronology in Pink Beam (2006) while focusing specifically on helping readers navigate the canon. Three major breaks in the sequence correspond to Dick's geographic relocations and personal life transitions.
page_summary: This document is a detailed chronology of Philip K. Dick's extant completed fiction, compiled and sequenced by Frank Hollander between July and November 2012. The chronology uses the Scott Meredith Literary Agency (SMLA) submission date as its primary organizing principle, treating the date Dick first substantially finished a work and sent it to his agent as the most reliable method for structuring the reading order of his fiction. Hollander acknowledges the chronology's similarity to Lord RC's chronology published in Pink Beam (2006) but distinguishes his project as focused specifically on establishing a coherent reading order for the complete extant fiction, excluding juvenilia. The document cross-references four major biographical and bibliographic sources: Paul Williams's Only Apparently Real (1986), the Collected Stories edited by Gregg Rickman and Paul Williams (1987), Gregg Rickman's To The High Castle (1989), and Lawrence Sutin's Divine Invasions (1989). The chronology identifies three major breaks in the sequence corresponding to significant shifts in Dick's geographic location and personal life, which roughly correspond with changes in marriage. The earliest works listed include 'Stability' (written 1947 or earlier) and the novel Gather Yourselves Together (dated uncertainly to 1949-50), with a note that at the time Dick joined the SMLA he had completed one novel and was beginning his second, Voices from the Street. The SMLA sequence proper begins on July 23, 1952, with 'The Builder' and 'Meddler,' followed by a dense burst of short story submissions through 1952 and 1953, including well-known works such as 'Paycheck,' 'Colony,' 'Second Variety,' 'The Golden Man,' 'The Father-Thing,' and many others. The document provides exact SMLA receipt dates for each work and includes Hollander's own analytical notes on sequencing decisions, making it an invaluable bibliographic tool for scholars and readers seeking to understand the chronological development of Dick's literary output.

display_title: Philip K. Dick Manuscript Collection Finding Aid (CSUF, SC.6, 2016)
author: CSU Fullerton University Archives & Special Collections
card_summary: The 2016 finding aid for the Philip K. Dick Manuscript Collection (Collection SC.6) held at California State University, Fullerton University Archives and Special Collections. The collection spans bulk dates 1959 to 1985 and comprises 14 document boxes. The finding aid describes access conditions, publication rights (property rights reside with CSUF), and provides researchers with an authoritative guide to the archival holdings of Dick's manuscripts. An essential reference tool for PKD scholars.
page_summary:
This is the 2016 finding aid for the Philip K. Dick Manuscript Collection (Collection SC.6) held at California State University, Fullerton (CSUF) University Archives and Special Collections. The collection spans bulk dates 1959 to 1985 and comprises 14 document boxes.

The finding aid specifies that the collection is open for research, though some materials are subject to access and reproduction restrictions. Property rights reside with CSUF, and no part of any manuscript may be quoted for publication without written permission from University Archives and Special Collections (800 N. State College, PLS-352, Fullerton, CA 92834-3599, phone 657-278-3444).

The CSUF Philip K. Dick Papers represent one of the two most important institutional repositories for Dick scholarship (alongside the Huntington Library holdings). The collection contains original manuscripts, correspondence, and related materials spanning Dick's career from the late 1950s through his death in 1982 and the subsequent management of his literary estate through 1985. This finding aid is an essential reference tool for any researcher planning to work with primary Dick materials, providing detailed descriptions of the collection's organization, access conditions, and the scope of holdings.

display_title: Philip K. Dick on Blade Runner (Starlog Magazine)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A Starlog magazine exclusive feature in which Philip K. Dick discusses the making of the Blade Runner film adaptation of his novel Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?. Dick recounts the tortuous history of the screenplay development, including false starts, dead ends, and multiple rewrites. He speaks candidly about his initial shock upon reading the original screenplay, comparing the experience to being condemned to eternal torture, as well as the uneasy alliance between his literary vision and the Hollywood production machine.
page_summary:
This Starlog magazine feature presents an exclusive interview with Philip K. Dick about the making of Blade Runner, the film adaptation of his novel Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?. The article, written by James Van Hise, captures Dick speaking with characteristic candor about the tortuous development of the screenplay.

Dick recounts the false starts, dead ends, and multiple rewrites that marked the Blade Runner screenplay's development, speaking with what the article describes as unflinching honesty. He describes his shock upon reading the original screenplay, comparing the experience to having died and been condemned to eternal torture. The article captures the uneasy alliance between Dick's literary vision and the Hollywood production machine.

The feature also references other Dick adaptations in development at the time: his 1966 short story "We Can Remember It for You Wholesale" was being produced at Walt Disney Studios from a Dan O'Bannon screenplay under the title Total Recall, and his 1953 story "Second Variety" had been adapted by O'Bannon for Virginia Palance and Capital Pictures. This interview is one of the last major interviews Dick gave before his death in March 1982, just months before Blade Runner's theatrical release, and is a key primary document for understanding Dick's relationship with Hollywood.

Dick Papers held in Special Collections at what appears to be California State University, Fullerton (referenced via library.fullerton.edu). Dick alongside materials related to Ray Bradbury, Frank Herbert, and the original Star Trek series. page_summary:
This document is a brief informational flyer or brochure about the Philip K. Dick Papers held in Special Collections at California State University, Fullerton (CSUF). The document describes Special Collections as housing the library's rarest and most valuable original materials.

Among the holdings referenced are the Boswell Collection on the History of Cartography, the Frederick and Patricia Shroyer Science Fiction, Fantasy, and Horror collection, and the James Boyer May (Amsberry-May) papers. The science fiction collections contain original manuscripts by Ray Bradbury, Philip K. Dick, and Frank Herbert, as well as materials related to the original Star Trek series.

The document directs researchers to finding aids available through the Online Archives of California (oac.cdlib.org) and the CSUF library catalog (library.fullerton.edu). CSUF's Special Collections is one of the most important institutional repositories for Dick scholarship, housing manuscripts, correspondence, and related materials that have been essential to biographical and scholarly work on Dick. The Dick Papers at Fullerton have been consulted by virtually every major Dick biographer and scholar.

display_title: Philip K. Dick Public Appearances Audit
author: Unknown
card_summary: A research document auditing the full scope of Philip K. Dick's documented public appearances across his life and career. Drawing on biographer Paul Williams's characterization of Dick as a 'reluctant convention-goer' and the introduction to The Last Interview and Other Conversations, the document establishes that Dick's public presence was concentrated primarily between 1972 and 1982, with very little documented activity during his most prolific publishing period of the 1960s. The audit begins with Dick's first major public lecture at the University of British Columbia in February 1972.
page_summary: This research document provides a systematic audit of the full scope of Philip K. Dick's documented public appearances across his life and career. Drawing on biographer Paul Williams's characterization of Dick as a 'reluctant convention-goer' and the introduction to The Last Interview and Other Conversations, the document establishes that Dick's public presence was concentrated primarily between 1972 and 1982, with virtually no documented activity during the 1960s when he published most of his major works. The audit begins with Dick's first major public lecture on February 17, 1972, 'The Android and the Human,' delivered at a luncheon at the University of British Columbia's Faculty Club in Vancouver, introduced by Professor Susan Wood's English department. Dick repeated this keynote the following day at VCON 2 at the Biltmore Hotel in Vancouver, where fewer than 100 people attended and Dick made headlines by announcing he intended to remain in Canada. The document covers the July 18, 1973 recorded talk at Cal State Fullerton with Professor Willis E. McNelly, who had persuaded Dick to donate his manuscripts to CSUF's Special Collections Library. Dick's appearance as Professional Guest of Honor at Westercon 27 in Santa Barbara (July 3-7, 1974) is noted. The most extensively documented appearance is Dick's September 24, 1977 speech at the Second International Science Fiction Festival in Metz, France, where he delivered 'If You Find This World Bad, You Should See Some of the Others,' declaring that 'we are living in a computer-programmed reality.' The audit records that the speech was poorly received, with Dick speaking in monotone and the interpreter's transcript differing from his actual words due to a last-minute rewrite. The document also covers 'How to Build a Universe That Doesn't Fall Apart Two Days Later' (1978), noting it was written in speech form but never actually delivered as a lecture. Multiple radio appearances in Vancouver and Los Angeles are catalogued, as are interviews conducted at the Metz festival by Uwe Anton, Werner Fuchs, Yves Breux, and Francis Luxereau.

display_title: Philip K. Dick Public Appearances Timeline (1955-1982)
author: Unknown
card_summary: A chronological timeline consolidating every documented instance of Philip K. Dick speaking publicly, appearing at conventions, giving interviews (in-person, radio, or television), or making other public-facing appearances across his lifetime (1928-1982). Each entry records the date, event description, type of appearance, location, and primary source documentation. The earliest documented appearance is a January 10, 1955 Oakland Tribune profile and interview, published when Dick was 26. The document confirms that the vast majority of documented appearances are concentrated in the final decade of Dick's life.
page_summary: This document presents a comprehensive chronological timeline consolidating every documented instance of Philip K. Dick speaking publicly, appearing at conventions, giving interviews, or making other public-facing appearances across his lifetime (1928-1982). Each entry records the date, event description, type of appearance (newspaper interview, formal speech, convention keynote, live radio broadcast, etc.), location, and primary source documentation. The earliest documented appearance is a January 10, 1955 Oakland Tribune profile and interview headlined 'Local Kid Makes Good,' published when Dick was 26 years old and beginning to gain recognition as a science fiction writer. The timeline confirms that during the 1960s, Dick's public life was 'essentially nothing,' despite this being his most prolific publishing period, with the 1963 Hugo Award for The Man in the High Castle at Discon I in Washington, D.C. noted without evidence that Dick attended the ceremony in person. The bulk of documented appearances fall between 1971 and 1982, beginning with interviews by a Norwegian journalist, Loren Cavit at Redwood High School in Larkspur, Goran Bengtson (a Swedish journalist visiting via the U.S. State Department), and Patrice Duvic (published in Revue Galaxie, No. 100, September 1972). The 1972 entries are particularly dense, documenting Dick's 'The Android and the Human' speech at UBC and VCON 2 in Vancouver, CBC and CKLG FM radio interviews, interviews at LACon I (the 30th Worldcon), and a three-hour KPFK FM live broadcast with Norman Spinrad focused on fascism in science fiction. The timeline notes the September 29, 1972 KPFK broadcast as a significant lost recording, identifying the Eric Foss Collection on Hour 25 (MS 054) at UC Riverside's Special Collections as the most promising lead for locating it. This resource is invaluable for scholars seeking to map Dick's engagement with the public sphere and trace the evolution of his public persona from reclusive pulp writer to celebrated intellectual figure.

display_title: Philip K. Dick Reviews Herman Wouk's The Caine Mutiny (Time, October 3, 1955)
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: A scanned single-page document identified by filename as Philip K. Dick's review of Herman Wouk's novel 'The Caine Mutiny', published in Time magazine on October 3, 1955. This represents a rare early piece of PKD non-fiction criticism, written during his most prolific short-story period. Dick's engagement with a major mainstream literary work like Wouk's Pulitzer Prize-winning novel is of biographical and critical interest. No text layer is present; content is image-only and requires manual transcription. 1 page.
page_summary:
A scanned single-page document identified as Philip K. Dick's review of Herman Wouk's novel The Caine Mutiny, published in Time magazine on October 3, 1955. No OCR text layer is present.

This represents a rare early example of Dick's work as a critic and reviewer rather than a fiction writer. A book review in Time magazine indicates a level of mainstream literary engagement that is unusual in Dick's biography, as he is primarily known as a science fiction writer who operated outside the literary establishment. The Caine Mutiny, Wouk's 1951 Pulitzer Prize-winning novel about a naval mutiny, deals with themes of authority, paranoia, and institutional power that resonate with Dick's own fictional preoccupations. This document provides valuable evidence of Dick's engagement with mainstream literature in the mid-1950s.

display_title: Philip K. Dick's Opus: Artifice as Refuge and World View (1975)
author: Darko Suvin
card_summary: Chapter 9 of a scholarly volume, originally published 1975, presenting a critical analysis of Philip K. Dick's literary opus under the title 'Philip K. Dick's Opus: Artifice as Refuge and World View'. The author divides Dick's writing into three main periods: 1952-62 (apprenticeship, culminating in The Man in the High Castle), 1962-65 (a 'high plateau' including Martian Time-Slip and Dr. Bloodmoney), and 1966-74. The chapter provides detailed close readings of individual novels and situates Dick within broader SF literary history. A foundational piece of early PKD academic scholarship. 60 pages.
page_summary:
This is Chapter 9 of a scholarly volume, originally published in 1975, presenting a foundational critical analysis of Philip K. Dick's literary opus. The author divides Dick's writing career into three main periods with detailed close readings of individual novels.

The first period (1952-62) is characterized as an apprenticeship, beginning with short stories (1952-56) and early novels from Solar Lottery to Vulcan's Hammer (1955-60), culminating in the mature polyphony of The Man in the High Castle (1962). The second period (1962-65) is identified as a "high plateau" comprising masterpieces including Martian Time-Slip and Dr. Bloodmoney, as well as The Three Stigmata of Palmer Eldritch, described as a "flawed but powerful near-masterpiece." The third period (1966-74) is characterized as "in many ways a falling off," marked by a turning from productive creative tension.

At 60 pages, this chapter represents one of the earliest and most substantial pieces of academic literary criticism devoted to Dick. The periodization framework it establishes has been influential in subsequent Dick scholarship, particularly the identification of the 1962-65 period as Dick's creative zenith. Published in 1975 during Dick's lifetime, it is a foundational document in the academic study of his work.

display_title: Philip K. Dick: Addresses and Residences Chronology
author: Unknown
card_summary: A reference document cataloguing every known address and residence of Philip K. Dick from his birth in Chicago in 1928 through his adult life in California. Each entry includes the years of residence, the full street address, and notes on significant life events or works associated with that location. The chronology covers Dick's birth at 7812 Emerald Avenue, Chicago; early childhood in Greeley, Colorado; years in Washington D.C.; relocation to Berkeley; and his adult residences including 2328 Telegraph Avenue and 1126 Francisco Street in Berkeley, where he lived with his wife Kleo.
page_summary: This reference document catalogues every known address, residence, and significant location associated with Philip K. Dick from his birth in Chicago in 1928 through his final years in Southern California. The chronology is organized in two sections: a primary table of residential addresses with years of residence, full street addresses, and notable works or life events associated with each location, followed by a supplementary table of significant non-residential locations including conference venues, educational institutions, literary settings, and other landmarks. The residential chronology begins with Dick's birth at 7812 Emerald Avenue, Chicago, in 1928 and traces his movements through Greeley, Colorado (219 13th Street, 1930s), Washington, D.C. (3039 Macomb Street NW, 1936-1938), and a series of Berkeley addresses including 1212 Walnut Street (1938), 1711 Allston Way (late 1930s, where he attended Berkeley High School), 1931 Dwight Way (1947-1948), 2328 Telegraph Avenue (1948-1952, where he worked at Art Music Company owned by Herb Hollis), and 1126 Francisco Street (1950-1958, where he married Kleo Apostolides). Later residences include Lorraine Avenue in Point Reyes Station (where he wrote The Man in the High Castle), 707 Hacienda Way in San Rafael (the communal household that inspired A Scanner Darkly), Cornwall Avenue in Vancouver (the X-Kalay rehabilitation period), 3028 Quartz Lane in Fullerton (where he wrote A Scanner Darkly), and his final residence at 408 E. Civic Center Drive in Santa Ana (where he wrote VALIS and The Divine Invasion). The document also includes supplementary material drawn from Sherry Gotlieb's account of her correspondence with Dick and her role as a script reader at United Artists, where her synopsis of Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep? led to the book's first option. References to Patricia Warrick's scholarly work and Frank Hollander's self-published edition of Dick's wartime adolescent writings further enrich this resource as a biographical reference tool.

display_title: Philip K. Dick: Essays of the Here and Now
author: David Sandner
card_summary: A volume of critical essays on Philip K. Dick edited or authored by David Sandner, published as part of McFarland's Critical Explorations in Science Fiction and Fantasy series edited by Donald E. Palumbo and C.W. Sullivan III. The series includes numerous academic studies of SF authors and themes. This collection focuses on Dick's engagement with themes of the present, reality, and everyday life. It sits within a rich tradition of McFarland scholarship on science fiction and fantasy literature.
page_summary:
Philip K. Dick: Essays of the Here and Now is a volume of critical essays edited by David Sandner, published by McFarland as part of their Critical Explorations in Science Fiction and Fantasy series, under the general editorship of Donald E. Palumbo and C.W. Sullivan III.

The text excerpt reveals an extensive list of companion volumes in the McFarland series, including Jennifer Simkins's The Science Fiction Mythmakers: Religion, Science and Philosophy in Wells, Clarke, Dick and Herbert (2016), which directly engages with Dick's religious and philosophical dimensions.

The collection's title signals its focus on Dick's engagement with present-tense reality rather than futuristic speculation. This framing reflects a key insight of Dick scholarship: that despite writing science fiction set in future worlds, Dick's fundamental concern was always with the nature of immediate, lived experience. His futures served as laboratories for testing questions about perception, authenticity, and the constructed nature of everyday reality.

As a McFarland academic publication, this volume represents the established institutional presence of Dick studies within the broader field of science fiction scholarship, confirming his canonical status in the academic study of science fiction.

display_title: Philip K. Dick: The Last Interview and Other Conversations
author: Philip K. Dick (interviews compiled by Melville House Publishing)
card_summary: A curated collection of interview transcripts with Philip K. Dick, published by Melville House in 2015. Includes 'Local Kid Makes Good' from the Oakland Tribune (1955), 'The Acid Truth' from Vertex (1974) by Arthur Byron Cover, 'Even Paranoids Have Enemies' by Cindy Lee Berryhill, 'Anticipating the Future' by D. Scott Apel (1987), 'Killing the Rat' by Charles Platt (originally in Dream Makers, 1980), and a conversation on Blade Runner and Hollywood. Spans nearly three decades of Dick's public interviews and conversations.
page_summary:
Philip K. Dick: The Last Interview and Other Conversations is a collection of interview transcripts published by Melville House in 2015. The volume assembles interviews spanning nearly three decades of Dick's public life.

The contents include: "Local Kid Makes Good" from the Oakland Tribune (January 10, 1955), capturing Dick at the very beginning of his career; "The Acid Truth" by Arthur Byron Cover from Vertex magazine (February 1974), conducted during the pivotal year of Dick's mystical experiences; "Even Paranoids Have Enemies" by Cindy Lee Berryhill, first published in Paul Williams's Only Apparently Real (Arbor House, 1986); "Anticipating the Future" by D. Scott Apel from Philip K. Dick: The Dream Connection (The Permanent Press, 1987); "Killing the Rat" by Charles Platt, originally published in Dream Makers: The Uncommon People Who Write Science Fiction (Berkley Books, 1980); "Blade Runner and Hollywood Temptations" by James Van Hise, expanded from a Star Log magazine interview (February 1982); and "Destruction and Enlightenment: The Last Interview" by Gregg Rickman, first published in Philip K. Dick: The Last Testament (Fragments West / The Valentine Press, 1985).

This collection is particularly valuable for its range, documenting Dick's evolving self-presentation from a young local author to a visionary philosopher grappling with Hollywood, metaphysics, and mortality.

page_summary:
A 10-page scanned document containing corrections, errata, or editorial notes relating to a collected edition of Philip K. Dick's short stories. No OCR text layer is present.

This document likely represents textual scholarship on Dick's short fiction, cataloging errors, variants, or corrections needed in one of the major collected editions of his stories (such as The Collected Stories of Philip K. Dick published by Citadel/Underwood-Miller). Textual accuracy in Dick's published works has been an ongoing concern for scholars and editors, as many of his stories were published under time pressure with minimal editorial oversight. This corrections document is a valuable resource for ensuring the fidelity of Dick's published texts to his original manuscripts.

display_title: PKD Exegesis Pages 1-6
author: Philip K. Dick
card_summary: Scanned handwritten pages 1 through 6 from Philip K. Dick's Exegesis, the sprawling private philosophical and theological journal he maintained from 1974 until his death in 1982. The text extraction is garbled OCR noise from Dick's handwritten manuscript, rendering the specific content illegible in digital form. As a scanned document of handwritten material, the actual content must be read from the original page images. This document is part of the broader Exegesis archive.
page_summary: This document contains scanned images of pages 1 through 6 from Philip K. Dick's Exegesis, the sprawling private philosophical and theological journal that Dick maintained from 1974 until his death in 1982. The Exegesis is widely regarded as one of the most extraordinary documents in American literary history -- an estimated 8,000 handwritten pages in which Dick attempted to interpret and systematize the mystical and visionary experiences he underwent beginning in February and March of 1974 (events he referred to collectively as '2-3-74'). The text extraction presented here is garbled OCR output, rendered illegible in digital form due to the extreme difficulty of performing optical character recognition on Dick's handwritten manuscript. The characters that appear in the extracted text are artifacts of the scanning process attempting to interpret Dick's handwriting, and they do not constitute readable content. As such, the substantive content of these six pages must be accessed through the original scanned page images rather than through the text layer. These opening pages of the Exegesis are of particular scholarly significance as they represent the beginning of Dick's sustained engagement with questions about the nature of reality, divine revelation, Gnostic cosmology, and the relationship between his fiction and his lived experience. The Exegesis has been partially published in edited form, most notably in The Exegesis of Philip K. Dick (2011), edited by Pamela Jackson and Jonathan Lethem, and the Zebrapedia Project has undertaken a collaborative transcription effort. These first six pages belong to the earliest stratum of the document, produced when Dick's visionary experiences were freshest, and they represent primary source material of the highest importance for understanding the intersection of Dick's metaphysical thought and literary imagination.

author: Various; compiled by unknown editor
card_summary: A 167-page compilation of Philip K. Dick's magazine interviews and related scholarly analysis. page_summary: This 167-page compilation assembles Philip K. Dick's magazine interviews alongside related scholarly analysis and bibliographic commentary. The document opens with an examination of 'Three Sci-Fi Authors View the Future,' published in Scholastic Voice on January 17, 1974, which featured Dick alongside Kurt Vonnegut and Michael Crichton. The compiler traces the piece's bibliographic history through Daniel J. H. Levack's PKD bibliography, the Stephensen-Payne and Benson updated bibliography Philip Kindred Dick: Metaphysical Conjurer, and Frank Bertrand's research, noting discrepancies in how the piece has been classified -- as nonfiction article, as interview, or as something in between. A letter from Dick to Ursula K. Le Guin dated February 2, 1974, in which Dick describes being 'interviewed by Voice, a magazine used in school,' is cited as evidence of the classification confusion. The compiler clarifies that the piece consists of short essays with pull quotes rather than a formal question-and-answer format. The Scholastic Voice section on Dick discusses The Three Stigmata of Palmer Eldritch, The Man in the High Castle, and Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep?, and includes Dick's reflections on science fiction's role as a guide for coping with the present environment rather than escapism. Dick expresses his view that humanity's destiny is tragic but can be met with dignity, courage, and humor. The compilation also includes 'Horselover Fat and The New Messiah,' a 1981 interview by John Boonstra published in the Hartford Advocate, in which Dick discusses the publication history of VALIS, explaining that he wrote two completely different versions of the novel and that the first version appears within the published book as the movie the characters watch. Dick reveals that the voice he associated with a 'transcendentally rational mind' had not spoken to him since writing The Divine Invasion. This compilation is a significant resource for understanding Dick's public self-presentation and his evolving views on his own work across the final decade of his life.

page_summary: This is issue 22 of PKD Otaku, a fan publication dedicated to Philip K. Dick, dated May 14, 2011. The editorial credits jami morgan, Frank Bertrand, and John Fairchild as the primary contributors, with additional work by regular editors Marc and Patrick. The table of contents reveals a rich and varied issue combining primary source material with fan scholarship and cultural commentary. The centerpiece is a transcription of 'An Interview With Philip K. Dick' by Yves Breux and Francis Luxereau, conducted at the Second International Science Fiction Festival (2eme Festival International de la SF) in Metz, France, between September 19 and 25, 1977, transcribed, edited, annotated, and with an afterword by Frank C. Bertrand. In this interview, Dick discusses the low status of science fiction writers in America, comparing it to his reception in France, where science fiction was taken seriously. He describes the constraints imposed by American publishers and libraries regarding sex, violence, and complex ideas, and recounts his origins as a pulp writer, noting that his largest American sale was Solar Lottery at over 300,000 copies. Dick credits the French reception of his work, beginning between 1964 and 1968, as the greatest stimulus to his development as a serious writer. Additional contents include a continued 'Kindred Spirit Interview' with jami morgan, an interview with Tessa Dick (Dick's fifth wife) by jami morgan, coverage of Tessa Dick on Paranormal Radio, Frank C. Bertrand's essay 'Late Night Thoughts, While Listening To Opera?', a second take on Scott Apel's Philip K. Dick: The Dream Connection, John Fairchild's informal review 'Philip K. Dick: Exhilaration and Terror of the Postmodern,' and notes and comments including a piece on Jared Loughner's 'World of Illusion' by Jack Shafer. The issue exemplifies the active and intellectually engaged fan scholarship community that has sustained interest in Dick's work and legacy.

author: William Sarill
card_summary: A 37-page academic paper by William Sarill of the New Genesis Institute, Arlington, MA, presented at the 33rd Annual Conference of the Society for Scientific Exploration on June 6, 2014. The paper examines whether PKD's fiction contains precognitive scientific information — ideas that anticipated later scientific discoveries — using Ubik as a primary case study. It surveys PKD's career of 44 novels and 121 stories, his autodidactic engagement with metaphysics and altered states of consciousness, and his central thematic preoccupations with the nature of reality and humanity.
page_summary:
"Precognitive Scientific Information in the Fiction of Philip K. Dick: Will the Real Ubik Please Stand Up?" is a presentation by William Sarill of the New Genesis Institute (Arlington, Massachusetts), delivered at the 33rd Annual Conference of the Society for Scientific Exploration on June 6, 2014.

The presentation examines instances in Dick's fiction where scientific concepts or discoveries appear to have been anticipated before they were formally recognized by the scientific community. The subtitle's reference to Ubik connects the paper to one of Dick's most celebrated novels, suggesting Sarill explores the prescient qualities of Dick's imaginative constructions. The Society for Scientific Exploration is an organization that investigates anomalous phenomena, and Sarill's paper situates Dick's fiction within the society's interest in precognition and unconventional modes of knowledge acquisition.

This presentation represents a distinctive strand of Dick scholarship that takes seriously the author's own claims about receiving information through non-ordinary channels, placing his literary work in dialogue with parapsychological research and frontier science.

page_summary:
The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick, 1973-1974 is published by Underwood-Miller with an introduction by William Gibson. Gibson describes the letters as "a harrowing literary journey" that is simultaneously "illuminating and embarrassing, brilliant and pathetic."

This period encompasses Dick's famous February-March 1974 mystical experiences ("2-3-74"), making these letters essential primary documents. Dick's own words from the letters reveal his characteristic blend of humor and metaphysical intensity, as in his December 9, 1974 reflection wondering if he is a reincarnation of Grace Slick and why he acquired paranormal powers in mid-March, followed by the quintessentially Dickian parenthetical: "(I'm kidding.) (I'm not kidding.) (Neither is true.) (Both are true.)"

Another passage from December 7, 1970 reveals existential weariness about the fate of his creative output, while a May 25, 1976 excerpt captures his humor about cosmic revelation. The volume stands as a crucial primary source offering unmediated access to Dick's thinking during the most transformative period of his life.

page_summary:
The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick, 1975-1976 continues the Underwood-Miller correspondence series, edited by Don Herron with an introduction by Tim Powers. Published in 1992 in trade and slipcased editions, the volume covers Dick's post-2-3-74 years.

Tim Powers, a close personal friend of Dick's during this period and himself a noted fantasy author, provides the introduction from firsthand knowledge. The frontispiece photograph is by Nicole Panter (1980). The publisher's note includes a significant caveat that the letters "cannot be considered reliable sources of information about any other persons," reflecting Dick's tendency toward paranoid speculation in correspondence.

The 1975-1976 period was one of intense creative and intellectual activity for Dick, as he worked simultaneously on the Exegesis and the novels that would become VALIS, The Divine Invasion, and The Transmigration of Timothy Archer. These letters provide essential context for understanding the development of his late metaphysical fiction.

SF Commentary ran for decades and published contributions from major critics and authors in the science fiction field. Gillespie's championing of Dick in its pages, alongside other authors like Ursula K. display_title: Statement by Stanislaw Lem Regarding Philip K. Dick
author: Stanislaw Lem
card_summary: A formal public statement by Polish science fiction author Stanislaw Lem responding to allegations made by Philip K. Dick, including accusations that Lem stole royalties from the Polish edition of Dick's novel UBIK. Lem expresses his general reluctance to see many of Dick's letters published, citing their damaging character and purely psychological rather than literary interest. He firmly denies the financial allegation, noting his admiration for UBIK, and addresses Dick's reported denunciations to law enforcement. A significant document in the troubled Dick-Lem correspondence and relationship.
page_summary:
This document is a formal public statement by the Polish science fiction author Stanislaw Lem responding to allegations made by Philip K. Dick. The statement addresses one of the most remarkable and troubling episodes in the history of science fiction literature: Dick's public accusations against Lem.

Lem opens by expressing his general view that many of Dick's letters should not be published, as they cast a bad light on their author and are of only psychological rather than literary interest. He notes Dick's curious way of repaying kindnesses and his political insinuations and denunciations to the police.

The central allegation Lem addresses is Dick's claim that Lem stole royalties from the Polish edition of Ubik. Lem explains that it was he who, out of admiration for the novel, recommended it to his own publisher Wydawnictwo Literackie in Cracow for publication. Lem addresses the financial accusations directly, defending his role in bringing Dick's work to Polish readers.

This document is significant for understanding the complex and troubled relationship between two of the twentieth century's most important science fiction writers. Dick's paranoid accusations against Lem, which included letters to the FBI alleging that Lem was part of a Communist conspiracy, represent one of the most dramatic manifestations of his documented paranoid tendencies. Lem's measured, sorrowful response provides essential context for scholars studying this episode.

The manuscript consists of 42 ribbon-copy typewritten pages and represents one of Dick's most important public addresses, delivered at the Vancouver Science Fiction Convention at the University of British Columbia in March 1972.

The speech is one of Dick's most significant non-fiction works, exploring the boundary between human and machine consciousness, the nature of authentic versus simulated experience, and the philosophical implications of increasingly sophisticated technology. These themes would continue to resonate through his later fiction, particularly Do Androids Dream of Electric Sheep? and its film adaptation Blade Runner.

The Ken Lopez catalog listing documents the manuscript's existence in the rare book market, providing provenance information valuable for scholars tracking the physical artifacts of Dick's literary career.
