The book opens with a comprehensive bibliography of Dick's published works from 1955 through the mid-1960s.
page_summary:
The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick: 1972-1973 is a volume in the selected letters series published by Underwood Books. At 423 pages, it is the largest volume in the series, reflecting the extraordinary volume and intensity of Dick's correspondence during this pivotal period.

The book opens with a comprehensive bibliography of Dick's published novels, beginning with Solar Lottery (1955) and continuing through A Handful of Darkness (1955), The World Jones Made (1956), The Man Who Japed (1956), Eye in the Sky (1957), The Cosmic Puppets (1957), The Variable Man (1957), Time out of Joint (1959), Dr. Futurity (1960), Vulcan's Hammer (1960), The Man in the High Castle (1962), The Game-Players of Titan (1963), The Penultimate Truth (1964), Martian Time-Slip (1964), The Simulacra (1964), Clans of the Alphane Moon (1964), The Three Stigmata of Palmer Eldritch (1965), and Dr. Bloodmoney, among others.

The years 1972-1973 were among the most dramatic in Dick's life: the mysterious break-in at his home in San Rafael, his trip to Vancouver for the science fiction convention, his brief period in Canada, his suicide attempt, his entry into the X-Kalay drug rehabilitation program, and his eventual relocation to Orange County, California. These letters provide essential primary documentation of one of the most turbulent and transformative periods in Dick's life.

display_title: Rosicrucian PKD Sources
author: Unknown (compiler)
card_summary: A 32-page research document compiling Rosicrucian and esoteric source materials relevant to Philip K. Dick's metaphysical thinking. Draws from the VALIS appendix (Tractates Cryptica Scriptura), referencing Apollonius of Tyana, Hermes Trismegistos, Paul of Tarsus, Simon Magus, Asklepios, Paracelsus, Boehme, and Bruno. Explores Dick's engagement with holographic universe theory, the concept of backward time movement, and the hermetic tradition.
page_summary:
Rosicrucian PKD Sources is a 32-page research document compiling esoteric and Rosicrucian source materials relevant to understanding Philip K. Dick's metaphysical framework. The document draws extensively from the VALIS appendix, the Tractates Cryptica Scriptura, beginning with Tractate 10 in which Apollonius of Tyana, writing as Hermes Trismegistos, states that 'that which is above is that which is below,' which Dick interprets as describing a holographic universe.

Tractate 11 presents what Dick calls the great secret known to Apollonius of Tyana, Paul of Tarsus, Simon Magus, Asklepios, Paracelsus, Boehme, and Bruno: that humanity is moving backward in time and the universe is contracting into a unitary entity. This compilation traces the lineage of esoteric thought that Dick drew upon, connecting his science fiction to centuries of hermetic, Rosicrucian, and Gnostic philosophical traditions.

The document is a valuable research tool for scholars investigating the sources of Dick's late metaphysical thought, particularly the syncretic blend of Gnosticism, hermeticism, Neoplatonism, and modern physics that characterizes the Exegesis and the VALIS trilogy. It demonstrates the depth and specificity of Dick's engagement with esoteric traditions that he wove into his fiction and philosophical writings.

Philip K. Dick: Contemporary Critical Interpretations is an edited collection of scholarly essays compiled by Samuel J. Umland and published by Greenwood Press, a major academic publisher. At 120 pages, the volume collects contemporary scholarly readings of Dick's fiction from multiple critical perspectives.

The PDF is a scanned document at 31.76 MB with no OCR text layer, indicating it was digitized from a physical copy. Greenwood Press (later absorbed into ABC-CLIO) was a prominent publisher of academic reference works and critical studies, and their publication of this volume reflects the institutional recognition of Dick as a significant literary figure worthy of sustained academic attention.

Umland's collection represents the state of Dick scholarship in the mid-1990s, a period when academic interest in Dick was accelerating rapidly. The volume's publication by an established academic press helped legitimize Dick studies within the broader field of literary criticism and provided a platform for diverse critical approaches to his work.

card_summary: The final volume of Dick's selected letters, covering 1980-1982, the last years of his life. ISBN 1-887424-26-1. At 344 pages, the volume opens with a passage describing Dick's second theophany in November (likely 1980), which he considered the most treasured moment of his life. page_summary:
The Selected Letters of Philip K. Dick: 1980-1982 is the final volume in the selected letters series, covering the last years of Dick's life. ISBN 1-887424-26-1. At 344 pages, the volume documents Dick's correspondence during his final creative period, which saw the completion of The Transmigration of Timothy Archer and the continuation of his massive Exegesis project.

The volume opens with an extraordinary passage in which Dick describes what he calls his second theophany, which he considers the most treasured moment of his life. He describes standing in his kitchen with his friend Ray Torrence (who is mentioned in Dick's story "Frozen Journey," also known as "I Hope I Shall Arrive Soon") at about eleven in the morning, as Torrence was about to return to Northern California after spending the night at Dick's apartment. Dick notes that this experience was self-authenticating and not something he discusses with even his closest friends.

This final volume of letters provides irreplaceable documentation of Dick's state of mind during the period when he was completing his last novel and continuing to process the mystical experiences that had consumed his intellectual life since 1974. The letters reveal a writer simultaneously at the height of his philosophical ambition and nearing the end of his life.

display_title: Upon the Dull Earth (Vocabulary Quiz / Study Guide)
author: Unknown (educator)
card_summary: A 5-page educational document containing a vocabulary quiz based on Philip K. Dick's short story "Upon the Dull Earth." The quiz presents passages from the story followed by multiple-choice vocabulary questions testing comprehension of words like "frantic," drawn from the story's atmospheric descriptions of Silvia and Rick encountering mysterious beings with distant flapping sounds like vast leaves rustling in a winter storm.
page_summary:
This is a 5-page educational document containing a vocabulary quiz based on Philip K. Dick's short story "Upon the Dull Earth." The quiz format presents passages from the story followed by multiple-choice questions testing vocabulary comprehension.

The opening passage describes Silvia's words being drowned out by distant flapping sounds, like vast leaves rustling in a winter storm, as the night sky comes alive with frantic poundings. The creatures are described as greedy and desperately eager, with flickers of fear touching the man (Rick) as he runs to catch up with Silvia. The first vocabulary question tests comprehension of the word 'frantic' with options including calm and relaxed, very worried or rushed with urgency, extremely shiny, and careless.

"Upon the Dull Earth" is one of Dick's more unusual short stories, blending domestic drama with cosmic horror and theological themes. The story involves beings that descend from the sky to consume offerings, and its title likely alludes to a passage from Shakespeare. This educational document demonstrates how Dick's work has entered the curriculum, being used as teaching material for vocabulary and reading comprehension instruction.
